title: Top 200 Drugs — NAPLEX Core List
description: The most commonly dispensed medications: brand/generic names, drug class, mechanism, indications, and key counseling points.
subject: pharmacy

cards.back: ACE inhibitor. Used for HTN, HF, post-MI, diabetic nephropathy. Counseling: take at same time daily, may cause dry cough (switch to ARB), avoid in pregnancy, monitor K+ and SCr

cards.front: Metoprolol succinate (Toprol-XL) vs metoprolol tartrate (Lopressor): difference
cards.back: Succinate: extended-release, taken once daily, used for HF and HTN. Tartrate: immediate-release, twice daily, used for acute MI and rate control
Dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker. Used for HTN and angina. Side effects: peripheral edema (ankle swelling), flushing, reflex tachycardia. Do not stop abruptly in angina

HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor. Take at any time (unlike simvastatin — evening preferred). Report unexplained muscle pain/weakness (myopathy risk). Avoid grapefruit juice with some statins. Check LFTs at baseline
Biguanide — decreases hepatic gluconeogenesis, improves insulin sensitivity. Take with food (reduces GI side effects). Hold before IV contrast. Avoid with eGFR < 30. Risk: lactic acidosis (rare)
Thyroid hormone replacement. Take on empty stomach 30–60 min before breakfast. Separate from calcium, iron, PPIs, cholestyramine by ≥ 4 hours (reduced absorption). Consistent brand preferred
Proton pump inhibitor. Take 30–60 min before first meal. Long-term use: ↓ B12 absorption, hypomagnesemia, C. diff risk, bone fracture risk, drug interactions (clopidogrel — CYP2C19 competition)
Short-acting β2-agonist bronchodilator (SABA). Rescue inhaler for acute bronchospasm. Shake before use, prime 4 sprays if unused > 2 weeks. Side effects: tachycardia, tremor, hypokalemia
Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) + LABA. Controller medication for asthma and COPD. Rinse mouth after use (prevent thrush). Black box: LABAs increase asthma-related death (use only with ICS)
β-lactamase inhibitor extends spectrum to β-lactamase-producing organisms (H. influenzae, Moraxella, S. aureus). Used for otitis media, sinusitis, bite wounds. Take with food (reduce GI upset)
Macrolide antibiotic. 5-day Z-pack: 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2–5. Take with or without food. Drug interactions: QT prolongation with antiarrhythmics. Finish entire course

Used for UTI, PCP prophylaxis, MRSA skin infections, nocardia. Adverse effects: hypersensitivity (sulfonamide allergy), hyperkalemia (blocks K+ secretion), nephrotoxicity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency

Tetracycline antibiotic. Take with full glass of water, remain upright 30 min (esophageal irritation). Avoid antacids/dairy/iron 2 hrs before and after. Sun protection (photosensitivity). Not for pregnancy or children < 8

Fluoroquinolone. Take 2 hrs before or 6 hrs after antacids, iron, calcium, dairy (chelation ↓ absorption). QT prolongation. Avoid in pregnancy, children. Tendon rupture risk (especially Achilles, elderly, corticosteroid use)

Vitamin K antagonist. Monitor INR (target 2–3 for most indications, 2.5–3.5 for mechanical valves). Narrow TI. Many interactions (CYP2C9 substrates). Vitamin K-rich foods (green leafy vegetables) can decrease effect. Patient should maintain consistent intake

cards.front: Apixaban (Eliquis): class, reversal, and advantage over warfarin
cards.back: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC), factor Xa inhibitor. Twice daily. No routine INR monitoring needed. Reversal: andexanet alfa. Advantage: fewer drug/food interactions. Renal dosing required
P2Y12 ADP receptor antagonist — irreversibly inhibits platelet aggregation. Hold 5 days before surgery. Interaction: PPIs (especially omeprazole) compete for CYP2C19 activation — reduced efficacy
Antiplatelet: 81 mg daily (irreversibly acetylates COX-1 → ↓ thromboxane A2). Analgesic/antipyretic: 325–650 mg. Anti-inflammatory: 3–6 g/day. Reye's syndrome: avoid in children with viral illness

Schedule II controlled substance. Max acetaminophen 4g/day (consider 3g in hepatic disease/elderly). Watch for sedation and constipation. Naloxone co-prescribing recommended for chronic opioid therapy

Schedule V in many states (formerly Rx-only). Anticonvulsant mechanism: binds voltage-gated Ca2+ channels (α2δ subunit). Uses: seizures, neuropathic pain, postherpetic neuralgia, hot flashes. Dose renally adjusted. Avoid abrupt discontinuation

cards.front: Sertraline (Zoloft) and other SSRIs: Black Box Warning
cards.back: Increased risk of suicidal thinking in children, adolescents, and young adults (up to age 24) — monitor closely in first few weeks. Discontinuation syndrome if stopped abruptly. Serotonin syndrome risk with MAOIs (washout 14 days)
Reduce max dose to 10 mg/day in elderly and hepatic impairment (standard max: 20 mg/day). Most selective SSRI, fewer drug interactions

SNRI (serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor). Used for MDD, GAD, diabetic peripheral neuropathy, fibromyalgia, chronic musculoskeletal pain. Hepatotoxicity risk — avoid in significant hepatic impairment or heavy alcohol use
Atypical antipsychotic. Used for schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, MDD adjunct. Metabolic effects: weight gain, hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia. QTc prolongation. Sedating (used off-label for insomnia)

Schedule IV CNS depressant (imidazopyridine — acts on BZD GABA receptor). Only for short-term insomnia. Take immediately before bed with 7–8 h remaining. Complex sleep behaviors (sleep-driving). Reduced dose for women and elderly

Schedule II. Blocks dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake. Used for ADHD. Sustained-release formulations: Concerta (18hr), Metadate (12hr). Monitor height/weight in children. Avoid in cardiovascular disease

Xanthine oxidase inhibitor → ↓ uric acid synthesis. Initiate prophylactic colchicine/NSAID (first 3–6 months — allopurinol can precipitate acute gout flare on initiation). Maintain hydration. HLA-B*58:01 screening in Asian patients before use (SJS risk)

Inhibits microtubule polymerization → inhibits neutrophil migration → reduces uric acid crystal-induced inflammation. Used for acute gout and prophylaxis. Side effects: diarrhea (dose-dependent), nausea, myelosuppression. CYP3A4 and P-gp substrate

Intermediate-acting glucocorticoid. 5 mg prednisone ≈ 5 mg prednisolone ≈ 4 mg methylprednisolone ≈ 0.75 mg dexamethasone. Taper if used > 1–2 weeks (HPA suppression → adrenal insufficiency if abruptly stopped)

Bisphosphonate. Take on empty stomach with 8 oz water. Remain UPRIGHT for ≥ 30 minutes (prevents esophageal ulceration). Weekly dosing (70 mg). Avoid calcium/antacids within 30 min. Osteonecrosis of jaw (rare)

cards.front: Insulin glargine (Lantus) vs insulin lispro (Humalog): when to administer
cards.back: Glargine (basal): once daily, same time each day, no peak. Lispro (bolus): 0–15 min before meals (or immediately after, especially if meal intake uncertain)

cards.front: Metoprolol tartrate: maximum single dose and frequency for HTN
cards.back: 25–100 mg twice daily (up to 450 mg/day). IV form: 5 mg q5min × 3 doses for acute rate control
Monitor: serum K+ (risk of hyperkalemia, especially with ACE inhibitors/ARBs/K+ supplements), BUN/SCr (renal function). Check in 1–2 weeks after initiation or dose change
PO: onset 30–60 min. IV: onset 5 min. 'Ceiling dose' concept: double dose if inadequate response. Monitor K+, Mg2+, Na+, SCr, uric acid. Ototoxicity with rapid IV infusion
Disulfiram-like reaction with ALCOHOL (avoid during treatment and 48h after). Inhibits CYP2C9 → ↑ warfarin levels (monitor INR). Used for C. diff (PO), anaerobes, BV, trichomoniasis

Antibiotic-associated diarrhea and C. difficile colitis (highest risk of all antibiotics for C. diff). Used for anaerobic infections, dental infections, skin/soft tissue, MRSA skin infections (topical)

cards.front: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu): when to start and renally adjusted dosing
cards.back: Start within 48 hours of symptom onset (or ASAP if high risk/hospitalized). 75 mg twice daily × 5 days. Renal adjustment: CrCl < 30 → 75 mg once daily. Post-exposure prophylaxis: 75 mg once daily × 10 days
Concomitant nitrate use (both ↑ cGMP → severe hypotension). Also avoid with alpha-blockers (hypotension), riociguat, and sGC stimulators

α1A antagonist. Used for BPH (improves urinary flow). Take 30 min after same meal daily. Side effects: orthostatic hypotension, abnormal ejaculation, floppy iris syndrome (inform surgeon before cataract surgery)
5α-reductase inhibitor → ↓ conversion of testosterone to DHT. Proscar (5 mg): BPH. Propecia (1 mg): male-pattern baldness. Teratogenic in pregnant women (Category X, do not handle crushed tablets)

5-HT1B/1D agonist → vasoconstriction + inhibits pain neurotransmitter release. Used for acute migraine and cluster headache. Contraindicated: CAD/CVD, uncontrolled HTN, within 24h of another triptan or ergotamine

5-HT3 antagonist antiemetic. Dose-dependent QT prolongation — 32 mg IV single dose no longer recommended. Avoid in patients on QT-prolonging drugs. Oral dissolving tablet is preferred. Safe in pregnancy for NVP

Leukotriene receptor antagonist for asthma, allergic rhinitis. Black Box Warning: serious neuropsychiatric adverse reactions (depression, suicidal ideation, behavioral changes). Use only if benefit > risk
Longest half-life of SSRIs (~2–6 days; active metabolite norfluoxetine 4–16 days) → less discontinuation syndrome. Can be dosed every other day for taper. Most drug interactions of SSRIs

cards.front: Naloxone (Narcan): dose and route for opioid overdose
cards.back: 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/SC (repeat q2–3 min up to 10 mg). Intranasal: 4 mg. Onset: 2 min (IV). Duration: 30–90 min — shorter than most opioids → may need repeated doses or infusion. Auto-injector and intranasal kit available without Rx

cards.front: Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone): role in opioid use disorder
cards.back: Buprenorphine: partial μ-opioid agonist + κ-antagonist. Naloxone: added to deter IV misuse (poor sublingual absorption). Used for medication-assisted treatment. Schedule III. Ceiling effect on respiratory depression (safer than methadone)

Monitor: serum lithium (target 0.6–1.2 mEq/L), SCr, TFTs, CBC. Narrow TI: toxicity at > 1.5 mEq/L (tremor, ataxia, confusion) → > 2.0 (severe: seizures, coma). NSAIDs, thiazides, ACE inhibitors ↑ lithium levels

cards.front: Methotrexate: mechanism, indications, and key toxicity monitoring
cards.back: Inhibits DHFR → ↓THF → ↓purine/pyrimidine synthesis. Uses: RA, psoriasis, ectopic pregnancy, ALL, choriocarcinoma. Toxicity: myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity (methotrexate polyglutamates accumulate), mucositis, pulmonary toxicity. Leucovorin (folinic acid): rescue therapy. Monitoring: CBC, LFTs, SCr, CXR. Contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogen). High-dose requires urinary alkalinization + leucovorin rescue.

cards.front: Calcineurin inhibitors: cyclosporine vs tacrolimus — mechanism and toxicity
cards.back: Both inhibit calcineurin → ↓IL-2 → ↓T-cell activation. Cyclosporine: binds cyclophilin; tacrolimus: binds FKBP12 (tacrolimus = FK506). Both: nephrotoxicity, hypertension, opportunistic infections. Cyclosporine: hirsutism, gingival hyperplasia. Tacrolimus: more potent, ↑ neurotoxicity, ↑ glucose intolerance (diabetogenic). Both: major CYP3A4 substrates (many drug interactions).

cards.front: Digoxin: mechanism, indications, toxicity, and interactions
cards.back: Inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase → ↑intracellular Na+ → ↑Ca2+ → ↑cardiac contractility (positive inotrope) + ↑vagal tone (negative chronotrope). Indications: HFrEF, rate control in AF. Toxicity: narrow TI — nausea/vomiting, yellow-green halos, arrhythmias (especially with hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia). Treatment of toxicity: digoxin-immune Fab (Digibind). Amiodarone/verapamil: ↑ digoxin levels.
